input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. It consists of lower‑level and upper‑level chassis structures together with autonomy elements (perception, planning, and actuation modules). The vehicle runs an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) function on resource‑constrained on‑board electronic control units (ECUs), making it an appropriate candidate for off‑loading computation to a digital twin. The test‑bed operates in a laboratory setting where vehicle state, sensor feeds, and actuation commands can be observed and manipulated remotely.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three concrete services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface streams real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks; the UI also allows users to view, save, and generate reports of collected data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed calculation, and desired‑speed generation) is executed in the DT (e.g., cloud infrastructure) rather than on the vehicle’s ECUs, with the resulting desired speed sent back for actuation. (3) Remote Management – through the UI operators can enable/disable ACC, manually set the desired speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command. These services require low end‑to‑end latency communication and respect the safety‑critical nature of the system. Target users are operators (who monitor and manage ACC) and developers (who use monitoring and data‑collection capabilities for troubleshooting).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation uses Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) as the middleware for publish/subscribe communication, NVIDIA JetPack 5.0 as the software development kit for GPU‑accelerated processing, and MATLAB/Simulink for model‑based design of the ACC controller. Cloud resources provide the compute node for off‑loaded ACC execution, and low‑latency network links (e.g., DDS over Ethernet) satisfy safety‑critical timing requirements. The overall architecture follows the ISO 23247 digital‑twin framework for automotive applications.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Device coordination requires a shared notion of time; the system mandates clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms (the limit imposed by hardware) to support computation offloading between devices.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DT is decomposed into three top‑level ISO 23247 entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each instantiated on a separate hardware component, yielding a decentralized, entity‑based hierarchy rather than a single monolithic twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference model, mapping each functional entity (FE) to an individual ROS 2 package; each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s functionality. Entities are organized as Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User entities, with the User Interface FE split into visualization and control‑panel sub‑components, and a Data Storage component that does not align to any FE. This ROS 2‑based, entity‑centric deployment coordinates the software components across the vehicle hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin uses a geometric point‑cloud model of the vehicle environment generated from LiDAR and depth‑camera data. Behavioral models for object tracking are implemented with a DBSCAN clustering algorithm followed by a Kalman‑filter based state estimator. Data are ingested from the physical sensors, pre‑processed in ROS2 nodes, and stored in a relational SQL database that provides persistence for historic trajectories and configuration parameters. The model hierarchy links the raw point‑cloud layer to the clustered object layer and then to the tracked object layer, enabling the Digital Twin Entity to provide both static geometry and dynamic motion predictions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor status, steering servo positions) is captured by the Data Pre‑Processing FE, then passed through the Data Translation FE which serializes the streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. The resulting messages are transmitted from the vehicle to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer and are routed inside the DT via a ROS 2 middleware network. The point‑cloud data is pre‑processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component before being visualised. No explicit transmission frequency or event trigger is stated, but data are sent continuously for real‑time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin sends control commands back to the vehicle, including the desired speed computed by the off‑loaded ACC algorithm, ACC enable/disable flags, speed set‑points, and emergency‑brake commands. Commands are formatted by the Data Translation FE (serialization, unit conversion) and transmitted over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link to the vehicle’s actuation FE, where they are applied by the motor and steering servo actuators. The vehicle’s response (updated vehicle state) is fed back to the DT for confirmation. Specific validation, authentication or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are not described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑entity communication between the physical and digital twins uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP as the transport protocol. Internally, software components within the DT communicate via a ROS 2 publish/subscribe network. The architecture relies on the ISO 23247 Data Translation functional element to ensure consistent message formats. The system requires low end‑to‑end latency for ACC remote management, but exact bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy or security protocol specifications are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. In the architectural design phase the team first enumerates the key software components (Table I) and defines their relationships to the sensors and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2). The components are then allocated to Functional Entities (FEs) and each FE is realized as an independent ROS 2 package. Every ROS 2 package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s functionality. This mapping yields three high‑level entities: the Physical Twin Entity (handling data pre‑processing, device communication and point‑cloud clustering & tracking), the Digital Twin Entity (responsible for data‑management and control logic), and the User Entity (hosting the User Interface FE, which is split into a 3D‑visualisation component and a control‑panel component). Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware platform, providing clear ownership of functionality. The approach does not describe explicit version‑control, testing, or quality‑assurance practices beyond the adoption of the ISO 23247 structure and ROS 2 implementation.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built on the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, using its entity‑based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). Functional Entities are implemented as ROS 2 packages, aligning with the ROS 2 ecosystem standards. For time synchronization, the design follows vehicular safety‑critical standards that demand clock synchronization tighter than 100 ms; the implementation targets a 100 ms synchronization accuracy due to hardware limitations.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the system as a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle with lower and upper chassis levels and an ACC function. It correctly notes the motivation for offloading computation from resource-constrained ECUs. However, it uses somewhat generic phrasing like 'advanced sensor and control technology' rather than the specific technical details present in the text (e.g., specific LiDAR or Jetson models). It also mentions a 'laboratory setting' which is not explicitly stated in the provided text, though 'test-bed' is a fair description. Quotes: '1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU)'

The extraction is highly accurate and detailed, perfectly matching the three services defined in Section III.A of the source. It correctly lists the functionalities of Data Monitoring (UI, reports), Computation Offloading (ACC logic in the cloud/DT), and Remote Management (commands). It also accurately includes the latency requirements and the distinction between operators and developers. Quotes: 'Data Monitoring... user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'offloading only the ACC system', 'Remote Management... enabling/disabling ACC'

While the extraction correctly identifies ROS 2, NVIDIA JetPack 5.0, and the ISO 23247 framework, it contains unsupported details. The text links MATLAB/Simulink to 'point cloud clustering and tracking' rather than explicitly stating it is for the 'ACC controller' design. Furthermore, the extraction mentions 'DDS over Ethernet' for low-latency links, whereas the source text explicitly states 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

The description correctly identifies the three entity types defined in the architecture and their deployment on separate hardware components, reflecting the decentralized nature described. Quotes: 'User Entity... Digital Twin Entity... and User Entity', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'This mapping helps organize the SW components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247'.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate summary of the architecture, including the mapping of Functional Entities to ROS 2 packages, the specific decomposition of the User Interface FE, and the alignment (or lack thereof) of specific components. Quotes: 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'User Interface FE has been split into two sub-components: visualization and control panel', 'one SW component, Data Storage did not have a naturally aligned FE'.

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the models and data pipeline, including the specific algorithms (DBSCAN, Kalman filter), data storage (SQL database), and middleware (ROS2). This is strongly supported by Table I and Section III.B. Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the documents provide relevant information. Section III-B mentions a 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and a lower chassis 'responsible for controlling the steering and the speed'. Table I and Fig. 2 describe the 'Actuation' component which 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' and corresponds to the 'Actuation FE'. Fig. 2 also lists 'Motor' and 'Steering Servo' as components of the Physical Twin Entity.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the documents explicitly describe the sensing infrastructure. Section III-B lists 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. Figure 2 also visually displays these sensors ('LiDAR', 'Vehicle State') feeding into the system.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate as the source does not contain specific technical details regarding model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error correction mechanisms for this specific system. While the term 'high-fidelity' appears in the general definition of Digital Twins, the system-specific details required by this characteristic are absent.

detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.

hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,

functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the data flow from physical sensors to the digital twin, referencing specific data types (LiDAR, vehicle state) and components (Data Pre-Processing FE, Data Translation FE). It correctly states the use of Wi-Fi, UDP, and ROS 2, and mentions point cloud preprocessing. This is well-supported by quotes: 'This component [Data Translation] serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions' and 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U[DP]'. It misses the specific 100ms latency requirement mentioned in R7 but remains highly specific and detailed.

The description correctly identifies control commands (desired speed, enable/disable, emergency brake) and their path through the Data Translation FE and Wi-Fi UDP link to the Actuation FE. It accurately notes the absence of validation/conflict resolution details. block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
